modules.title: From migration to liberation
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 39, segment 1 through PDF p. 43, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the people in the story and state the arc plainly: Jacob's family went to Egypt, grew into a people, was enslaved and harmed, cried out, and was heard. Pause after oppression before describing liberation so the emotional movement is intelligible.

modules.title: Pesach, matzah, maror, and lived memory
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 53, segment 3 through PDF p. 55, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Point to each object as it is named and explain it before asking anyone to read: the offering recalls deliverance, matzah recalls hurried departure, and maror recalls the bitterness of slavery. Then connect the symbols to the teaching that every generation enters the story personally.

modules.title: Questions, memory, and family history
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 7, segment 3 through PDF p. 8, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that asking is part of the ritual and that this Haggadah tells Exodus through songs, images, and discussion rather than a single continuous script. Invite each person to choose one concrete family or Passover memory and allow anyone to pass.

modules.title: Women's resistance and the Exodus
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 13, segment 2 through PDF p. 15, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the arc in plain language: the ancestors reached Egypt, a new ruler enslaved their descendants, Shifra and Puah resisted orders to kill newborn boys, Moses later confronted Pharaoh, plagues struck Egypt, and the people left. Acknowledge Egyptian suffering and explain the cup-diminishing action.

modules.title: Cumulative closing songs
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 31, segment 2 through PDF p. 33, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are playful cumulative songs sung near the end. Teach one response before starting, make singing optional, and stop at a natural verse if the table is ready to close.

modules.title: Why this story is retold
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 5, segment 1 through PDF p. 5, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Maggid is the storytelling section and that the story belongs to the whole table. Invite people to take turns reading, while allowing anyone to pass.

modules.title: Four ways of approaching the story
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 6, segment 2 through PDF p. 6, segment 6
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce these as four modes that can exist within any person, not fixed labels for actual children. Explain unfamiliar spiritual vocabulary and do not ask guests to identify one another.

modules.title: A compact Torah story
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 8, segment 1 through PDF p. 8, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the main people and sequence before reading: ancestors migrate, descendants are enslaved, oppression intensifies, the people cry out, and liberation begins. Clarify that the text is a compact scriptural retelling rather than the whole story.

modules.title: Oppression, cry, and responsibility
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 9, segment 4 through PDF p. 10, segment 4
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the traditional verses linger over what oppression does to families and how a cry becomes heard. Invite one concrete question about whose cries society notices today, without requiring personal disclosure or making unsupported current claims.

modules.title: Grace, enoughness, and peace
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 18, segment 3 through PDF p. 18, segment 7
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that Barech is gratitude after eating and invite guests to notice sustenance, interdependence, and the wish for peace. State whether the table will read all four paragraphs or one selected portion.

modules.title: Korekh and survivor-led liberation
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 14, segment 3 through PDF p. 14, segment 5
modules.beginnerContext: Before making the Hillel sandwich, explain that Korekh combines bitter maror and sweet charoset; offer a content note for trafficking testimony and tell guests they may pass on the reading.

modules.title: Tzafun: seek what is hidden
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 19, segment 1 through PDF p. 19, segment 4
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that Tzafun means hidden and is the moment when the afikoman returns; frame the school and restaurant campaign as dated examples of turning learning into action, not as instructions for a current campaign.

modules.title: Birkat Hamazon and a life of dignity
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 21, segment 1 through PDF p. 21, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce Barech as the blessing after the meal and explain that this reading interprets one traditional plea for sustenance and shelter through the lens of coerced labor.

modules.title: Four Children: from awareness to agency
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 21, segment 3 through PDF p. 21, segment 4
modules.beginnerContext: First explain that the traditional Haggadah imagines four different kinds of questioners; this adaptation uses them as stages in learning about forced labor and finding a useful response.

modules.title: Karpas and nourishment for a long journey
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 28, segment 1 through PDF p. 28, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests to take a green vegetable, dip it in salt water, and taste it; then explain that the source connects this first dipping to both tears and the Israelites' search for drinkable water after crossing the sea.

modules.title: From spring festival to Freedom Seder
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 2, segment 2 through PDF p. 3, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce this as the source author's account of how Passover imagery traveled through Jewish, Christian, Muslim, and Black freedom traditions, and explain that the sequence ends with the first 1969 Freedom Seder.

modules.title: Three tastes of transformation
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 17, segment 1 through PDF p. 17, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Place the matzah, bitter herb, and charoset where everyone can see them; tell guests when to lift and taste each item and define charoset as the sweet fruit-and-nut mixture on the Seder plate.

modules.title: Why liberation is told
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 3, segment 1 through PDF p. 4, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the Haggadah as a guide to a participatory Passover meal and explain that the story moves between ancient slavery and present responsibility. Invite guests to take turns reading and allow anyone to pass.

modules.title: Questions opening the telling
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 18, segment 1 through PDF p. 18, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that asking questions is a ritual act and that Maggid is the storytelling section. Give the Four Questions a clear reader cue, then say in plain language why the group turns from questions to the Exodus story.

modules.title: Four children and four questions
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 19, segment 3 through PDF p. 21, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the figures as four ways people may approach a difficult story, not labels for actual children. Translate unfamiliar terms, make reading roles optional, and give the table one strong follow-up question rather than asking guests to classify themselves.

modules.title: Oppression, departure, and diminished joy
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 24, segment 1 through PDF p. 25, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: State the story arc before reading: the Israelites were enslaved, Moses confronted Pharaoh, plagues struck Egypt, and the people left. Explain the cup-diminishing action and say that freedom is not celebrated by ignoring Egyptian suffering.

modules.title: Crossing, Miriam, and gratitude
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 26, segment 1 through PDF p. 27, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the story reaches the sea, where escape becomes possible and Miriam leads song. Give guests a singing or spoken-response option, then introduce Dayenu as a gratitude chain rather than a celebration of anyone's destruction.

modules.title: Preparing people, objects, and expectations
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 3, segment 1 through PDF p. 3, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Use this before the Seder to explain what guests will read, eat, ask, and do. Add a simple checklist naming the plate, matzah, wine or grape juice, salt water, basin, towels, and optional additions.

modules.title: Questions that open the story
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 18, segment 1 through PDF p. 23, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that Maggid is the section where the table tells the Exodus story and that questions are part of the ritual. Assign short readings around the table, let guests pass, and give younger participants concrete prompts.

modules.title: Slavery, courage, and the road out
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 33, segment 1 through PDF p. 37, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the main figures and state the full arc: a ruler enslaved the Israelites, resistance and courage kept hope alive, Moses confronted Pharaoh, plagues brought suffering, and the people escaped. Pause for compassion before celebrating freedom.

modules.title: Plate symbols and an inclusive table
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 44, segment 1 through PDF p. 45, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Point to each named plate object and explain its practical use before its symbolism. If an orange or other optional item appears, say why this table chose it and give an accurate history.

modules.title: Carrying freedom beyond the table
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 69, segment 1 through PDF p. 70, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Nirtzah closes the formal order but sends the work of freedom into the year ahead. Offer one final shared line and one optional reflection rather than introducing a new discussion.

modules.title: First cup in the reconstructed rite
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 2 through official source snapshot, segment 4
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that this is a reconstructed ancient Land of Israel form of the first-cup blessing, then tell guests whether to read the English, Hebrew, or both.

modules.title: Questions leading into servitude
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 8 through official source snapshot, segment 10
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the Four Questions as observations about what makes this night different, and explain that the response begins the story of oppression and the cry for freedom.

modules.title: From ancestral beginnings to the plagues
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 11 through official source snapshot, segment 15
modules.beginnerContext: Before reading, give a short spoken bridge: the Haggadah widens the Exodus story by tracing the family’s earlier migrations, enslavement, cry, and divine response.

modules.title: Praise after the freedom story
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 19 through official source snapshot, segment 21
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that the story now shifts into poetry: the table responds to liberation with Psalms and a historical liturgical composition.

modules.title: A place and a voice at the table
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 5 through official source snapshot, segment 6
modules.beginnerContext: Invite each guest to put one personally meaningful object near their place, and explain that people may take turns reading, ask questions, request slower speech, or pass.

modules.title: The Four Questions with answers
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 14 through official source snapshot, segment 16
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the Four Questions are traditionally asked by the youngest willing participant but may be shared by anyone; point to the matzah, maror, dipping foods, and reclining as each is named.

modules.title: A compact Exodus narrative
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 23 through official source snapshot, segment 25
modules.beginnerContext: Before reading, briefly explain that Joseph had earlier helped Egypt survive a famine and that the Israelites' descendants remained there; this supplies the one missing setup before the source begins with the new Pharaoh.

modules.title: Chanted ancestry, oppression, and cry
modules.sourceLocation: authoritative odt text, segment 6 through authoritative odt text, segment 8
modules.beginnerContext: Give guests the Exodus arc in clear English before this selection: the family migrated, grew, was enslaved, cried out, and was heard.

modules.title: Plagues leading into Dayenu
modules.sourceLocation: authoritative odt text, segment 9 through authoritative odt text, segment 12
modules.beginnerContext: Frame the plagues with sorrow for suffering, tell guests how to diminish the cup, and explain that Dayenu shifts from catastrophe toward gratitude.

modules.title: Closing cumulative songs
modules.sourceLocation: authoritative odt text, segment 37 through authoritative odt text, segment 40
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are playful cumulative songs traditionally sung at the end; provide a refrain cue and make participation optional.

modules.title: Preparing by removing leaven
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 2, segment 1 through PDF p. 2, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that searching for and nullifying hametz is a pre-Passover practice, not something guests must improvise at the table. Give a short practical definition of leaven and clearly mark this as optional preparation.

modules.title: The traditional Four Children
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 12, segment 1 through PDF p. 13, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce the figures as inherited teaching types, not judgments about actual children or guests. Explain the historical language, avoid assigning labels around the table, and give a gentler discussion prompt.

modules.title: From ancestry to oppression
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 15, segment 1 through PDF p. 19, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: State the narrative arc before the detailed verses: the family went to Egypt, became a people, and was enslaved and harmed. Explain that the people's cry and liberation continue in the following part of Maggid. Introduce each scriptural quotation with one sentence of plain-language meaning.

modules.title: Plagues with diminished joy
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 22, segment 1 through PDF p. 25, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the plagues are catastrophes within the liberation story, name the cup-diminishing custom, and say explicitly that the table does not celebrate civilian suffering. Use wine or grape juice and give one action cue before the list.

modules.title: Opening grace after the meal
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 42, segment 1 through PDF p. 45, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that Barech is the gratitude said after eating and tell guests whether the leader will read or everyone will respond. Offer a shortened path without implying that a mixed-belief guest must affirm every theological line.

modules.title: Translator's critical historical frame
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 5, segment 1 through PDF p. 5, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Identify this as the 2025 translator's introduction to a 1919 socialist parody, not part of the original ritual; tell guests that the warning is required before any other excerpt from this source.

modules.title: Maror, freedom, and equality
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 27, segment 2 through PDF p. 33, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the text substitutes Freedom, Equality, and Socialism into familiar Maror and Hallel formulas; distinguish this historical parody from a blessing or creed endorsed by the app.

modules.title: Wisdom for the journey and four cups
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 3, segment 1 through PDF p. 4, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the traditional Seder has four cups and an additional cup for Elijah; this source dedicates the cups to four connected social problems and the Elijah cup to building Dr. King's Beloved Community.

modules.title: Candles between the fires
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 6, segment 2 through PDF p. 7, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Before lighting candles, warn that the reading names lynching, Auschwitz, Hiroshima, gun violence, and climate disaster; invite anyone to step out, pass, or join only for the final candle blessing.

modules.title: Miriam's fish
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 2, segment 1 through PDF p. 2, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: After explaining the traditional Passover offering, identify the fish as an optional addition honoring Miriam, water, prophecy, and women's role in the Exodus; provide a non-food or plant-based symbol if needed.

modules.title: Leeks before Dayenu
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 3, segment 1 through PDF p. 3, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Before Dayenu, explain the Persian Jewish custom and ask consent before any gentle scallion tap; guests may hold or wave a leek instead, and no one should be struck or pressured.

modules.title: Rav Huna's beets before washing
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 4, segment 1 through PDF p. 4, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Before the second handwashing, identify beets as an optional Ashkenazi food and explain that the text uses their lasting red color to discuss inherited teaching and covenant.

modules.title: Maxwell House and Diaspora satire
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 6, segment 1 through PDF p. 6, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Before Tzafun, identify this as an optional comic reading about Jewish advertising history and the Maxwell House Haggadah; explain the PaRDeS method and make all coffee or after-dinner drinks optional and caffeine-free if desired.

modules.title: Festival sanctification and Exodus memory
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 3, segment 7 through PDF p. 4, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that this is an extended traditional sanctification passage linking Passover to the Exodus and festival calendar. Offer it only to a table seeking substantial liturgy, define the sea and vengeance imagery, and follow with the simpler wine blessing from a separate verified module.

modules.title: Abraham challenges the idols
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 13, segment 7 through PDF p. 14, segment 8
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a traditional expansion about young Abraham challenging his father's idols and the ruler Nimrod, not part of the basic Exodus outline. Name the characters, summarize the missing transitions, and use it only with an adult or well-facilitated table.

modules.title: An Exodus poem from suffering to redemption
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 31, segment 3 through PDF p. 31, segment 6
modules.beginnerContext: State the narrative arc before the poem: the Israelites suffered slavery, liberation came through destructive plagues, they crossed the sea, and the liturgy turns toward future celebration. Frame firstborn and enemy-death imagery with sorrow and keep Temple hopes within inherited historical liturgy.

modules.title: A Hasidic closing story
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 55, segment 1 through PDF p. 55, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that Nirtzah closes the formal Seder and that this is a historical Hasidic story about longing and readiness. Define the military conscription context, identify the quoted Zion language as inherited in the story, and make the long reading optional.

modules.title: Western Yiddish build-well song
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 60, segment 2 through PDF p. 60, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a Western Ashkenazi closing song whose traditional refrain hopes for the Temple's rebuilding. Give a transliteration and one repeated response, explain the historical liturgical meaning, and let guests listen instead of sing.

modules.title: Who knows one?
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 62, segment 1 through PDF p. 62, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests this is a cumulative number song near the end of the Seder. Teach the question-and-response pattern, clarify unfamiliar references only when they first appear, and stop at a natural number if time is short.

modules.title: A multilingual Chad Gadya guessing game
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 64, segment 1 through PDF p. 64, segment 4
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that this is an optional language game based on the final Chad Gadya verse, not a required ritual. Read the ordinary English pattern first, divide guests into teams only if they want to play, and avoid assuming knowledge of any language.

modules.title: Chad Gadya call and response
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 74, segment 3 through PDF p. 76, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Chad Gadya is a playful cumulative end song and teach the repeated line before beginning. Make singing optional, explain that the chain becomes violent in a folktale-like way, and allow an early stop for children or time.

modules.title: A medieval English Seder survives
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 1 through official source snapshot, segment 3
modules.beginnerContext: Present this as a short historical note before the ritual begins: a 1287 English Jewish manuscript preserves local Seder customs from before the expulsion.

modules.title: Distinctive medieval Maggid readings
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 12 through official source snapshot, segment 15
modules.beginnerContext: Label this explicitly as a scholar’s comparison of manuscript readings, and summarize the relevant difference before displaying any Hebrew excerpt.

modules.title: A preserved closing poem
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 28 through official source snapshot, segment 30
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the article preserves a medieval poetic addition sung near the end of the Seder, then provide an editorially checked translation or refrain guide.

modules.title: Preparing for the Seder night
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 10, segment 1 through PDF p. 11, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Summarize in modern English that the source begins with removing leaven, evening prayers, returning home, and approaching the prepared table.

modules.title: The duty to tell and the Four Children
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 15, segment 1 through PDF p. 17, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Explain that the text moves from the obligation to retell the Exodus into four different learning postures; use inclusive modern labels outside the exact source excerpt.

modules.title: Oppression, cry, and redemption
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 20, segment 2 through PDF p. 24, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Provide a concise English story first: forced labor intensified, the people cried out, their suffering was seen, and the text turns toward deliverance.

modules.title: Hebrew-Latin Dayenu witness
modules.sourceLocation: official open siddur dayenu transcription, segment 1 through official open siddur dayenu transcription, segment 4
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce Dayenu as a cumulative gratitude song and make clear that this selection is a historical Hebrew-Latin transcription, not the app’s English sing-along version.

modules.title: Opening a home Seder
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 3, segment 1 through PDF p. 5, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that this is an 1896 Reform home service with period-specific gender roles and God language. Name the first cup as wine or grape juice, summarize the gathering’s purpose, and invite readers to substitute inclusive titles or pronouns before reading the historical prayer.

modules.title: From migration to Moses's demand
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 6, segment 1 through PDF p. 7, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce this as the opening of one historical retelling, define unfamiliar biblical names as needed, and make clear that this excerpt moves from a migrating family to enslavement and Moses's demand for freedom.

modules.title: Freedom joined to responsibility
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 17, segment 1 through PDF p. 20, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a long 1896 meditation arguing that liberation carries social responsibility and needs just law; tell guests that dated passages have been omitted.

modules.title: Memory, influence, and closing resolve
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 40, segment 1 through PDF p. 44, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Name this as a historical closing meditation about how people live on through memory and influence, followed by scripture and a communal resolve.

modules.title: Teaching styles and ancestral memory
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 11, segment 2 through PDF p. 13, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: Explain the Four Children as different ways of engaging, use inclusive modern labels outside the exact source, and then introduce the move backward into ancestral history.

modules.title: Migration, oppression, cry, and deliverance
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 14, segment 1 through PDF p. 17, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Before reading, give the story in one English sentence: Jacob’s family migrated to Egypt, grew, was enslaved, cried out, was heard, and moved toward freedom through signs and plagues.

modules.title: Portuguese cumulative closing songs
modules.sourceLocation: PDF p. 40, segment 1 through PDF p. 42, segment 1
modules.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are cumulative end-of-Seder songs, provide a simple refrain and an English synopsis, and make Portuguese participation optional.

modules.title: Cyclical history and an interfaith opening
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 1 through official source snapshot, segment 2
modules.beginnerContext: State that this is an opt-in Battlestar fan adaptation, explain the cyclical-history premise, and label the Gâyatrî Mantra as a Hindu text rather than Jewish liturgy.

modules.title: Second cup and fictional migration story
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 5 through official source snapshot, segment 7
modules.beginnerContext: Give the real Exodus story elsewhere in full; introduce this only as the fan Haggadah’s fictional second-cup story about exile, migration, danger, and searching for home.

modules.title: Third cup and responsibility for creation
modules.sourceLocation: official source snapshot, segment 8 through official source snapshot, segment 9
modules.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a fictional ethical meditation: the characters face responsibility for creating life and for the consequences they tried to disown.
